mechanism_narrative: Sustained monthly payroll growth above 150,000 persons signals robust labor demand and consumer income expansion, which drives equity valuations higher through increased corporate earnings expectations and consumer spending. This always-on trend mechanism predicts positive S&P 500 returns over 20 trading days when PAYEMS growth remains positive.
evidence_refs: fact:PAYEMS at 135354.0 thousand as of Jan 2012 indicates ongoing post-crisis employment recovery
regime_hint: post-shock recovery with positive labor momentum
failure_condition: SPX produces negative 20-day returns in more than 55% of months where PAYEMS shows month-over-month growth, or Rank IC between PAYEMS growth and forward returns falls below 0.05 over any 2-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: When the federal funds rate remains below 1.0%, the zero-lower-bound regime creates a persistent liquidity premium that suppresses equity risk premia and inflates asset prices. This threshold-triggered mechanism activates only when FEDFUNDS crosses below 1.0% and predicts positive S&P 500 returns over 60 trading days due to sustained monetary accommodation.
evidence_refs: fact:FEDFUNDS at 5.24% as of Jan 2012 indicates non-ZLB regime, but historical context shows ZLB periods 2008-2015
failure_condition: During periods when FEDFUNDS < 1.0%, SPX produces negative 60-day returns in more than 50% of triggered instances, or correlation between FEDFUNDS level and forward 60-day returns is not significantly negative (p > 0.10).

mechanism_narrative: Rising retail sales (RSAFS) combined with accelerating housing starts (HOUST) indicates broad-based demand expansion across consumer and construction sectors. This interaction-driven mechanism requires both series to show positive momentum simultaneously, signaling robust aggregate demand that lifts corporate earnings and equity valuations over the next 5 trading days.
candidate_trigger: RSAFS month-over-month growth > 0.5% AND HOUST month-over-month growth > 2.0%
evidence_refs: fact:RSAFS at 367941.0M and HOUST at 1850.0K as of Dec 2011 show moderate demand conditions
regime_hint: late-cycle expansion with synchronized demand acceleration
failure_condition: Rank IC of the interaction signal (RSAFS growth × HOUST growth) with 5-day forward returns falls below 0.04 over any 18-month rolling window, or the signal produces positive returns in fewer than 58% of triggered instances.

mechanism_narrative: Declining industrial production (INDPRO) signals weakening supply-side capacity utilization and potential margin compression for equities. This threshold-triggered mechanism activates when INDPRO shows a 3-month decline exceeding 1.5%, predicting negative S&P 500 returns over 20 trading days as investors anticipate earnings deterioration.
evidence_refs: fact:INDPRO at 113.2096 as of Dec 2011 reflects post-crisis industrial recovery
failure_condition: SPX produces positive 20-day returns in more than 52% of instances where INDPRO 3-month decline exceeds -1.5%, or Rank IC between INDPRO changes and forward returns is not significantly negative (p > 0.10).

mechanism_narrative: Rising consumer price inflation (CPIAUCSL) combined with elevated unemployment (UNRATE) creates stagflationary pressure that erodes real equity returns and increases discount rates. This interaction-driven mechanism activates when inflation accelerates while unemployment remains above 4.5%, predicting negative S&P 500 returns over 60 trading days.
evidence_refs: fact:CPIAUCSL at 202.3 and UNRATE at 4.8% as of Dec 2011 show modest inflation with elevated unemployment
regime_hint: stagflationary regime with conflicting monetary pressures
failure_condition: Rank IC of the stagflation interaction signal (CPIAUCSL growth × UNRATE level) with 60-day forward returns falls below 0.05 over any 2-year rolling window, or the signal produces negative returns in fewer than 55% of triggered instances.

mechanism_narrative: Real GDP growth (GDPC1) measured quarterly serves as the primary demand signal. When GDPC1 shows quarter-over-quarter growth above 2.0% annualized, it signals sustained economic expansion that supports equity valuations. This always-on trend mechanism predicts positive S&P 500 returns over 60 trading days during expansion periods.
evidence_refs: fact:GDPC1 at 11385.3B (2017 dollars) as of Q3 2011 indicates ongoing recovery
regime_hint: economic expansion with positive real growth
failure_condition: SPX produces negative 60-day returns in more than 50% of quarters where GDPC1 annualized growth exceeds 2.0%, or Rank IC between GDPC1 growth and forward returns falls below 0.06 over any 3-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Unemployment rate (UNRATE) rising above 5.0% signals labor market deterioration and reduced consumer confidence, which depresses equity valuations through lower expected earnings and higher risk premia. This threshold-triggered mechanism activates when UNRATE crosses above 5.0%, predicting negative S&P 500 returns over 20 trading days.
evidence_refs: fact:UNRATE at 4.8% as of Jan 2012 remains below 5.0% threshold
regime_hint: labor market deterioration or recession onset
failure_condition: SPX produces positive 20-day returns in more than 55% of months where UNRATE exceeds 5.0%, or Rank IC between UNRATE level and forward returns is not significantly negative (p > 0.10).

mechanism_narrative: Housing starts (HOUST) serve as a leading indicator of construction demand and forward economic activity. When HOUST declines more than 10% quarter-over-quarter, it signals weakening housing demand and reduced forward GDP growth expectations, predicting negative S&P 500 returns over 5 trading days as investors reprrice growth expectations downward.
evidence_refs: fact:HOUST at 1850.0K as of Dec 2011 reflects housing market stabilization post-crisis
regime_hint: housing demand shock or construction cycle downturn
failure_condition: SPX produces positive 5-day returns in more than 60% of instances where HOUST 3-month decline exceeds -10.0%, or Rank IC between HOUST changes and forward returns is not significantly negative (p > 0.10).
